whenToUse: Type chosen at runtime from user input, config, or environment
Creation logic duplicated across multiple classes
You want to add new types without touching existing client code
Building a library/SDK that others extend with their own types
whenNotToUse: Type is known at compile time and is stable
Only 2 stable types -- simple if-else is fine
Creation is trivial (just new X() with no logic)
You're adding it 'for the future' that may never come

structure.Factory: One place that creates the right product (NotificationFactory)
structure.Client: Uses products through interface, never touches concrete classes

cheatSheet.sixWordSummary: Ask Factory. Don't new it yourself.
cheatSheet.codeSmellTrigger: if-else / switch on type to decide which object to new

bestPractices: Use enums for factory keys -- compile-time safety, no typo crashes
Simple Factory for 80% of cases -- don't over-engineer with Factory Method unless creation is complex
Factory Method only when creation involves private knowledge per type (config loading, key management)
Store Supplier/lambdas in registry, not instances -- lazy creation avoids resource waste
Products should be self-sufficient when possible -- factory stays clean
Make factory injectable (interface, not static) for testability
Return new instances for stateful products -- never cache mutable objects
Parse strings to enums at system boundaries -- enums travel through business logic

commonMistakes.mistake: Creating a Factory for only 1 type that won't change
commonMistakes.correct: Just use 'new' directly. Add Factory when you actually have 3+ types chosen at runtime.

commonMistakes.mistake: Factory knows private details of every product (SMTP config, API keys, tokens)
commonMistakes.correct: Products self-configure, or use Factory Method so each subclass owns its private knowledge.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using raw strings as factory keys -- typos crash at runtime
commonMistakes.correct: Use enums. Parse strings to enums at API/config boundaries only.

commonMistakes.mistake: Returning cached mutable objects from factory -- thread-unsafe shared state
commonMistakes.correct: New instance for stateful products. Cache only stateless/immutable products.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using Factory Method when there's no shared orchestration in the abstract class
commonMistakes.correct: If the abstract class only has createProduct() and nothing else, Simple Factory is cleaner.

commonMistakes.mistake: Confusing Factory (WHICH type to create) with Builder (HOW to configure complex object)
commonMistakes.correct: Factory picks the type in one call. Builder configures step-by-step. Combine when you need both.
